name: Google
overview: Google is a multinational technology company specializing in Internet-related services and products. Known for its rigorous interview process focusing on algorithmic problem-solving and system design.
hiringProcess: Google's hiring process typically involves an online assessment, followed by phone screens, and then on-site interviews consisting of 4-5 rounds covering coding, system design, and behavioral questions.
eligibility: Strong CS fundamentals, competitive programming background preferred. GPA > 3.5 for new grads.
preparationTips: Focus on LeetCode medium/hard problems, practice system design, study Google's engineering culture and values.

interviewQuestions.title: Tell me about yourself
interviewQuestions.content: Craft a compelling 2-minute pitch covering your background, key achievements, and why you're interested in Google.
interviewQuestions.expectedAnswer: Structure: Present → Past → Future. Start with current role, highlight relevant achievements, connect to why Google excites you.
interviewQuestions.sampleAnswer: I'm a software engineer with 3 years of experience building scalable distributed systems at [Company]. I led the redesign of our real-time data pipeline that reduced latency by 40% and handles 1M events/sec. I'm drawn to Google because of the scale of problems you solve and the opportunity to impact billions of users.
tips: Show genuine interest in Google's mission

interviewQuestions.title: Explain OOP Principles
interviewQuestions.content: Explain the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming with real-world examples.
interviewQuestions.expectedAnswer: Cover Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism with code examples.
interviewQuestions.sampleAnswer: The four pillars are: 1) Encapsulation - bundling data and methods, controlling access via private/public. 2) Abstraction - hiding complexity, exposing only necessary interfaces. 3) Inheritance - creating hierarchies for code reuse. 4) Polymorphism - same interface, different implementations.
followUp: When would you prefer composition over inheritance?

interviewQuestions.title: Design a URL Shortener
interviewQuestions.content: Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly that can handle millions of URLs.
interviewQuestions.expectedAnswer: Cover requirements, API design, database schema, encoding algorithm, caching, and scaling strategies.
interviewQuestions.sampleAnswer: Requirements: shorten URLs, redirect, analytics. Use base62 encoding with counter/hash. Store in NoSQL (DynamoDB). Cache hot URLs in Redis. Use CDN for redirects. Handle 100M URLs/day with horizontal scaling.
followUp: How would you handle custom aliases?

interviewQuestions.title: What are your salary expectations?
interviewQuestions.content: How to handle salary expectation questions professionally.
interviewQuestions.expectedAnswer: Research market rates, provide a range, emphasize total compensation.
interviewQuestions.sampleAnswer: Based on my research and experience level, I'm looking at a total compensation in the range of $X-$Y, but I'm flexible and more focused on the role's growth potential and impact.

problems.title: Two Sum
problems.statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

bruteForce.approach: Check all pairs using nested loops

optimal.approach: Use a hash map to store complement values

problems.interviewTips: Always clarify if the array is sorted. Mention the trade-off between time and space complexity.

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.statement: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals.

examples.explanation: Since intervals [1,3] and [2,6] overlap, merge them into [1,6].

optimal.approach: Sort by start time and merge greedily

problems.interviewTips: Sorting is key. Always ask about edge cases: empty array, single interval, all overlapping.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.statement: Design a data structure that follows the constraints of a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache.

examples.input: LRUCache(2), put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)
examples.explanation: Cache evicts key 2 when capacity is reached.

optimal.approach: HashMap + Doubly Linked List for O(1) operations

problems.interviewTips: Explain why Map maintains insertion order in JS. In other languages, discuss doubly linked list + hashmap approach.

experiences.lessons: Focus on communication during problem solving. Google values how you think, not just the final answer.
experiences.timeline: Applied → OA (1 week) → Phone Screen (2 weeks) → Onsite (3 weeks) → Offer (2 weeks)
